1	---
2	title: DDx Server
3	weight: 4
4	---
5	
6	`ddx server` is a lightweight Go web server that exposes your document library, bead tracker, document graph, agent session logs, and personas over HTTP and MCP endpoints — with an embedded web UI for visual management.
7	
8	## Quick Start
9	
10	```bash
11	# Start the server (reads from current project)
12	ddx server
13	
14	# Custom port
15	ddx server --port 3000
16	
17	# Open in browser
18	open http://127.0.0.1:8080
19	```
20	
21	## Demo
22	
23	<video controls autoplay muted loop width="100%" style="border-radius: 8px; border: 1px solid #e5e7eb;">
24	  <source src="/demos/ddx-server-ui.webm" type="video/webm">
25	  Your browser does not support the video tag.
26	</video>
27	
28	## Web UI
29	
30	The embedded web UI provides a browser-based interface for managing your DDx project. Six pages cover the full surface:
31	
32	| Page | URL | What it does |
33	|------|-----|-------------|
34	| **Dashboard** | `/` | Project overview: document count, bead status, stale docs, server health |
35	| **Documents** | `/documents` | Browse, search, filter by type, view rendered markdown, edit in-place |
36	| **Beads** | `/beads` | Kanban board with drag-and-drop, full-text search, create/claim/close/reopen |
37	| **Graph** | `/graph` | Interactive document dependency graph visualization |
38	| **Agent** | `/agent` | Agent session history with prompt/response/token details |
39	| **Personas** | `/personas` | Browse personas, view by role, inspect content |
40	
41	### Beads Kanban Board
42	
43	The beads page is a full project tracker with:
44	
45	- **Three-column kanban**: Open, In Progress, Closed
46	- **Drag-and-drop**: Move beads between columns to update status
47	- **Full-text search**: Server-side search via the GraphQL schema's cursor-paginated `beads` query with a `search:` argument
48	- **Create beads**: Modal form with title, type, priority, labels, description, acceptance criteria
49	- **Detail panel**: Click any bead to see full details, dependencies, and execution evidence
50	- **Bead actions**: Claim, unclaim, close, reopen with reason
51	
52	### Document Library
53	
54	- **Browse by type**: Filter documents by category (prompts, personas, patterns, etc.)
55	- **Search**: Find documents by name
56	- **Rendered markdown**: View documents with full GitHub-flavored markdown rendering
57	- **Edit in-place**: Switch to raw editing mode, save changes directly
58	
59	## HTTP API
60	
61	All data is available programmatically over REST:
62	
63	### Documents & Search
64	
65	| Method | Path | Description |
66	|--------|------|-------------|
67	| `GET` | `/api/documents` | List all library documents |
68	| `GET` | `/api/documents/:path` | Read document content |
69	| `PUT` | `/api/documents/:path` | Write document (auto-commits) |
70	| `GET` | `/api/search?q=<query>` | Full-text search across documents |
71	| `GET` | `/api/personas` | List all personas |
72	| `GET` | `/api/personas/:role` | Resolve persona for a role |
73	
74	### Bead Tracker
75	
76	| Method | Path | Description |
77	|--------|------|-------------|
78	| `GET` | `/api/beads` | List all beads |
79	| `GET` | `/api/beads/:id` | Show a specific bead |
80	| `GET` | `/api/beads/ready` | List ready beads (no open deps) |
81	| `GET` | `/api/beads/blocked` | List blocked beads |
82	| `GET` | `/api/beads/status` | Summary counts by status |
83	| `GET` | `/api/beads/dep/tree/:id` | Dependency tree |
84	| `POST` | `/api/beads` | Create a bead |
85	| `PUT` | `/api/beads/:id` | Update bead fields |
86	| `POST` | `/api/beads/:id/claim` | Claim a bead |
87	| `POST` | `/api/beads/:id/unclaim` | Release a claim |
88	| `POST` | `/api/beads/:id/reopen` | Reopen with reason |
89	| `POST` | `/api/beads/:id/deps` | Add/remove dependencies |
90	
91	### Document Graph
92	
93	| Method | Path | Description |
94	|--------|------|-------------|
95	| `GET` | `/api/docs/graph` | Full dependency graph |
96	| `GET` | `/api/docs/stale` | Stale documents |
97	| `GET` | `/api/docs/:id` | Document metadata and staleness |
98	| `GET` | `/api/docs/:id/deps` | Upstream dependencies |
99	| `GET` | `/api/docs/:id/dependents` | Downstream dependents |
100	| `GET` | `/api/docs/:id/history` | Document commit history |
101	
102	### Execution & Agent Sessions
103	
104	| Method | Path | Description |
105	|--------|------|-------------|
106	| `GET` | `/api/exec/runs` | List execution runs |
107	| `GET` | `/api/exec/runs/:id` | Show execution run detail |
108	| `GET` | `/api/exec/runs/:id/log` | Execution run logs |
109	| `POST` | `/api/exec/run/:id` | Dispatch an execution |
110	| `GET` | `/api/agent/sessions` | List agent sessions |
111	| `GET` | `/api/agent/sessions/:id` | Session detail |
112	
113	### System
114	
115	| Method | Path | Description |
116	|--------|------|-------------|
117	| `GET` | `/api/health` | Liveness check |
118	| `GET` | `/api/ready` | Readiness check |
119	
120	## MCP Endpoints
121	
122	AI agents can connect to `ddx server` via MCP (Streamable HTTP at `POST /mcp`). Available tools:
123	
124	**Documents:** `ddx_list_documents`, `ddx_read_document`, `ddx_search`, `ddx_resolve_persona`, `ddx_doc_write`, `ddx_doc_history`, `ddx_doc_diff`, `ddx_doc_changed`
125	
126	**Beads:** `ddx_list_beads`, `ddx_show_bead`, `ddx_bead_ready`, `ddx_bead_status`, `ddx_bead_create`, `ddx_bead_update`, `ddx_bead_claim`
127	
128	**Graph:** `ddx_doc_graph`, `ddx_doc_stale`, `ddx_doc_show`, `ddx_doc_deps`
129	
130	**Execution:** `ddx_exec_definitions`, `ddx_exec_show`, `ddx_exec_history`, `ddx_exec_dispatch`
131	
132	**Agent:** `ddx_agent_sessions`, `ddx_agent_dispatch`
133	
134	## GraphQL API
135	
136	AI clients and the web UI use the GraphQL endpoint at `POST /graphql`. The schema covers all types available via REST. Use [GraphiQL](http://127.0.0.1:8080/graphiql) to explore the schema interactively.
137	
138	## Architecture
139	
140	- **Single binary** — server and web UI (SvelteKit) are embedded in the `ddx` CLI
141	- **Stateless** — reads from filesystem on each request, no database
142	- **Localhost by default** — binds to `127.0.0.1` for security
143	- **File-backed** — all data comes from git-tracked files (`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, library docs, agent logs)
144	- **Web UI stack** — SvelteKit (Svelte 5, `adapter-static`) + GraphQL (Houdini client) + Bun; built to static files and embedded via `//go:embed`
145

1 --- 2 title: Architecture 3 weight: 3 4 --- 5 6 This page covers the concrete moving parts: how beads flow through the queue, 7 how `ddx work` drains it by composing `ddx try` on top of `ddx run`, how 8 personas attach to roles, and what the project-local install model means on 9 disk. For the layered run model in detail, see 10 [Run Architecture](../run-architecture/). 11 12 For exhaustive specifications, follow the `FEAT-*` references into 13 `docs/helix/01-frame/features/` in the repository. 14 15 ## Bead Lifecycle 16 17 A bead is a self-contained unit of work with an ID, title, description, 18 acceptance criteria, labels, and a dependency edge set. Beads live in the 19 project's bead store and move through a small set of states. 20 21 ``` 22 created → ready → in-progress → review → closed 23 ↘ blocked ↗ 24 ``` 25 26 - **created** — a bead exists but isn't pickable yet (often because deps 27 aren't closed). 28 - **ready** — all dependencies are closed; the bead is in the work queue. 29 - **in-progress** — an agent or developer has claimed it. 30 - **review** — implementation done; an automated or human review is checking 31 the acceptance criteria. 32 - **closed** — review passed; the bead is done. 33 - **blocked** — surfaced separately when something external prevents 34 progress. 35 36 `ddx bead ready` lists pickable work. `ddx bead status` shows the queue 37 shape. `ddx bead dep tree` visualizes the DAG. See **FEAT-004** for the bead 38 tracker spec. 39 40 ```mermaid 41 stateDiagram-v2 42 [*] --> created: ddx bead create 43 created --> ready: deps closed 44 created --> blocked: external blocker 45 ready --> in_progress: agent claims 46 in_progress --> review: implementation done 47 in_progress --> blocked: surfaces blocker 48 review --> closed: AC pass 49 review --> ready: AC fail (reopen, escalate) 50 review --> declined: push conflict /
cannot reconcile 51 blocked --> ready: blocker cleared 52 closed --> [*] 53 declined --> [*] 54 ``` 55 56 {{< callout type="info" >}} 57 **Why beads, not tickets?** Beads are local files in JSONL, not records in 58 an external tracker. They diff, they merge, they branch with the code. 59 Importing from `bd`/`br` keeps you portable. 60 {{< /callout >}} 61 62 ## The Run Architecture 63 64 DDx drains the bead queue through three layered run primitives. Each 65 higher layer composes the layer beneath it. 66 67 ``` 68 ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 69 │ ddx work — drain the bead queue │ 70 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ 71 │ iterates 72 ▼ 73 ddx try ── no ready beads ──▶ exit 74 │ 75 ▼ 76 create execution worktree from base rev 77 │ 78 ▼ 79 ddx run (cheap tier) with bead prompt 80 │ 81 ▼ 82 review by stronger model against acceptance criteria 83 │ 84 ┌────┴────┐ 85 pass fail 86 │ │ 87 ▼ ▼ 88 merge reopen bead, escalate to higher tier 89 │ │ 90 └────┬─────┘ 91 ▼ 92 next bead 93 ``` 94 95 The three layers — `ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx work` — are **cost-tiered by 96 design**: the implementer is a cheap model, the reviewer is a stronger one, 97 and deterministic checks sit above review catching what slipped 98 through. Failed reviews thread the findings into the next attempt's prompt 99 so the escalating model knows exactly what was missed. 100 101 `ddx work` is the user-facing queue-drain entry point. Under the hood it 102 iterates `ddx try `, which in turn wraps one or more `ddx run` 103 invocations. See [Run Architecture](../run-architecture/) for the layered 104 contract and **FEAT-006** (agent service) plus **FEAT-010** (run 105 architecture) for the underlying specifications. 106 107 ```mermaid 108 flowchart TD 109 Start(["ddx work"]) --> Claim{"ready bead
in queue?"} 110 Claim -- "no" --> Done(["exit"]) 111 Claim -- "yes" --> Worktree["create execution
worktree from base rev"] 112 Worktree --> Render["render bead prompt
(persona + context)"] 113 Render --> RunImpl["run implementer agent
(cheap tier)"] 114 RunImpl --> Review["review by stronger model
against acceptance criteria"] 115 Review --> Pass{"AC pass?"} 116 Pass -- "no" --> Escalate["reopen bead, thread findings,
escalate to higher tier"] 117 Escalate --> Claim 118 Pass -- "yes" --> Merge["merge to base"] 119 Merge --> Push{"push race?"} 120 Push -- "yes" --> Recover["auto-recover:
rebase / retry"] 121 Recover --> Merge 122 Push -- "no" --> Close["close bead"] 123 Close --> Claim 124 125 classDef cheap fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#1e293b; 126 classDef strong fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#a21caf,color:#1e293b; 127 class RunImpl cheap; 128 class Review strong; 129 ``` 130 131 ## Personas and Role Binding 132 133 A **persona** is a document that shapes how an agent behaves. DDx ships 134 personas like `code-reviewer`, `implementer`, `test-engineer`, and 135 `architect`. Each is a Markdown file with behavioral instructions. 136 137 A **role** is an abstract function — "the thing that reviews code", "the 138 thing that implements features". Projects bind specific personas to roles 139 in `.ddx.yml`: 140 141 ```yaml 142 persona_bindings: 143 code-reviewer: code-reviewer 144 architect: architect 145 implementer: cheap-implementer 146 ``` 147 148 When an agent is dispatched for a role, the bound persona is composed into 149 the prompt envelope along with bead context, project config, and any 150 relevant patterns. Swapping a persona is a one-line config change, not a 151 code change. 152 153 This separation is why the layered run architecture's cost tiering works 154 cleanly — the implementer role binds to a cheap-tier persona, the reviewer 155 role binds to a strong-tier one, and `ddx work` never hardcodes a model 156 name. 157 158 ```mermaid 159 flowchart LR 160 subgraph Workflow["Workflow (e.g. HELIX)"] 161 RoleR["role:
code-reviewer"] 162 RoleI["role:
implementer"] 163 RoleA["role:
architect"] 164 end 165 subgraph Project[".ddx/config.yaml"] 166 Bind["persona_bindings:
code-reviewer → code-reviewer
implementer → cheap-implementer
architect → architect"] 167 end 168 subgraph Library["library/personas/"] 169 PR["code-reviewer.md"] 170 PI["cheap-implementer.md"] 171 PA["architect.md"] 172 end 173 Envelope["prompt envelope
(persona + bead + context)"] 174 Agent["agent harness"] 175 176 RoleR --> Bind 177 RoleI --> Bind 178 RoleA --> Bind 179 Bind --> PR 180 Bind --> PI 181 Bind --> PA 182 PR --> Envelope 183 PI --> Envelope 184 PA --> Envelope 185 Envelope --> Agent 186 187 classDef cfg fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937; 188 class Bind cfg; 189 ``` 190 191 ## Project-Local Install Model 192 193 DDx installs are **project-local**. The only global artifact is the `ddx` 194 binary itself (and optionally `ddx-server`). Everything else lives under the 195 project root. 196 197 After `ddx init`: 198 199 ``` 200 / 201 ├── .ddx/ 202 │ ├── config.yaml # project DDx config 203 │ ├── plugins/ # installed plugins (local only) 204 │ │ └── ddx/ # the default DDx plugin (library) 205 │ ├── executions/ # ddx try / ddx work evidence 206 │ └── beads/ # bead store (JSONL) 207 ├── .agents/ 208 │ └── skills/ # agent-facing skills 209 └── .claude/ 210 └── skills/ # Claude-specific skill installs 211 ``` 212 213 `ddx install ` only writes under those three trees. There is no 214 `~/.ddx`. There is no `ddx install --global`. Cloning the repo gives a 215 collaborator the entire DDx surface for the project; deleting `.ddx/` 216 removes it. 217 218 This is a deliberate inversion of the usual CLI pattern. It makes: 219 220 - **Reproducibility** — the repo is the source of truth. 221 - **Onboarding** — `git clone && ddx doctor` is enough. 222 - **Cleanup** — reversible by file deletion. 223 224 See **FEAT-015** for the installation architecture spec. 225 226 ```mermaid 227 flowchart TB 228 subgraph Global["Global (developer machine)"] 229 Bin["ddx binary
(~/.local/bin/ddx)"] 230 Server["ddx-server
(optional, only global artifact)"] 231 end 232 subgraph Project["<projectRoot>/ — everything else lives here"] 233 direction LR 234 DDxDir[".ddx/
config.yaml · plugins/ ·
executions/ · beads/"] 235 AgentsDir[".agents/skills/"] 236 ClaudeDir[".claude/skills/"] 237 end 238 239 Bin -->|"ddx init"| Project 240 Bin -->|"ddx install <plugin>"| DDxDir 241 Bin -->|"ddx install <plugin>"| AgentsDir 242 Bin -->|"ddx install <plugin>"| ClaudeDir 243 244 classDef global fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#4b5563,color:#1f2937; 245 classDef local fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e293b; 246 class Bin,Server global; 247 class DDxDir,AgentsDir,ClaudeDir local; 248 ``` 249 250 ## How the Pieces Fit 251 252 The bead tracker decides **what** to do. The persona system decides **how** 253 the agent should approach it. The three-layer run architecture 254 (`ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work`) runs the agent and routes results. The 255 project-local install model means all of that is in the repo, not in a 256 developer's home directory or a remote service. 257 258 That's the whole platform. Phases, gates, and methodology live one layer up 259 in HELIX; deterministic verification lives in the check runner. 260

ddx:
  id: FEAT-023
  depends_on:
    - helix.prd

    - FEAT-010

# Feature: Multi-Machine Sync

**ID:** FEAT-023
**Status:** Implemented
**Priority:** P1
**Owner:** DDx Team

## Overview

`ddx sync` is a first-class CLI command that synchronizes DDx-managed files
(`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/plugins/`) with `origin/main`
in a single canonical flow. It exists in both one-shot and watch (daemon)
modes and is constrained to never touch any path outside the DDx-managed
allowlist.

Sync solves the multi-machine drift problem operators see when DDx is checked
out on more than one machine: workers churn `beads.jsonl` every few minutes
during execute-loop drains, the working tree is constantly dirty in tracked
paths, and manually pulling/stashing/popping/committing/pushing is friction
that gets skipped, causing tracker divergence.

## Problem Statement

**Current situation:** Operators with DDx on multiple machines manually run
the sequence `git fetch && git stash && git pull && git stash pop && git add
.ddx/beads.jsonl && git commit -m "tracker" && git push` (with execution
evidence variants) several times per session to keep tracker state aligned.
A 2026-04-29 dogfood session ran this flow ~6 times in ~3 hours.

**Pain points:**

- Manual sync is constant friction; missing a sync risks divergent tracker
  state between machines.
- No remote-only solution exists: cloud routines cannot touch a local
  working tree, and a server-side sync only helps if every endpoint is the
  server (DDx is per-project local).
- Operators forget to sync, then have to reconcile divergent
  `beads.jsonl` lines by hand.

**Desired outcome:** A single `ddx sync` keystroke (or zero, with
`--watch`) performs the canonical flow safely. Anything that cannot be
auto-resolved — stash-pop conflict, double push failure, divergent base —
aborts cleanly with a structured non-zero exit, writes a failure record,
and surfaces through `ddx doctor` so the operator notices without having
to remember to look.

## Scope

### In Scope

- `ddx sync` one-shot command running the canonical flow:

# Feature: Server Node State and Project Registry

**ID:** FEAT-020
**Status:** In Progress
**Priority:** P1
**Owner:** DDx Team

## Overview

The `ddx server` process acquires a stable node identity at startup, persists
its state across restarts, and maintains a registry of projects it is serving.
CLI commands that touch stateful resources (beads, agent sessions, doc graph)
automatically register the calling project with the running server so the
server always has an accurate picture of active work on the machine.

This feature is the foundation for FEAT-021 (multi-node dashboard UI) and the
eventual coordinator model where multiple ddx-server instances on different
machines forward state to a shared upstream.

## Problem Statement

**Current situation:** `ddx server` starts with no identity and no memory.
Each start is stateless. There is no machine-level concept; the server cannot
distinguish one host from another, and it cannot register projects it didn't
start in. CLI commands that interact with beads or agents have no path to
surface their activity to the server UI.

**Pain points:**
- The server UI can only see the project it was started in; projects registered
  by other CLI invocations are invisible
- Restarting the server loses all context about what was running
- There is no stable identifier for a node that a future coordinator can use
  to aggregate state across machines
- The UI has no way to answer "what is this server, and what projects is it
  tracking?"

**Desired outcome:** Every `ddx server` instance has a node name and stable ID
derived from the hostname (or `DDX_NODE_NAME`). When it starts, it loads state
from the previous run. As projects are registered — either by the server itself
at startup, or by CLI commands running in other terminals — the server builds
an accurate project registry. The server writes an address file so CLI commands
can find it without configuration.

## Architecture

### Node Identity

```
node name  →  DDX_NODE_NAME env  >  os.Hostname()  >  "unknown"
node id    →  sha256(name)[:4], prefix "node-", stable across restarts
```

# Feature: Multi-Node Dashboard UI

**ID:** FEAT-021
**Status:** In Progress
**Priority:** P1
**Owner:** DDx Team

## Overview

Extends the FEAT-008 web UI with a node/project-aware dashboard. The UI can
show a combined view of beads and DDx run activity across all registered
projects on a node, or narrow to a specific project for context-dependent
views (artifact browser, dependency graph, run history, commit log). Node,
project, and page are all embedded in the URL so every view is bookmarkable
and shareable.

The dashboard UI is implemented as a SvelteKit application with graphql-request for
GraphQL data fetching. Every page uses `+page.ts` load functions to fetch data
from the `/graphql` endpoint defined in SD-022.

## Problem Statement

**Current situation:** The FEAT-008 UI is implicitly scoped to the single
project the server was started in. There is no node concept, no project picker,
and no combined cross-project view for beads or layer-aware run history.

**Pain points:**
- Operator cannot see all active work across projects in one dashboard
- Navigating to a different project requires restarting the server in that
  project's directory
- Deep links to a specific bead, artifact, or run record are not stable because the URL
  carries no project context
- Queue-drain evidence is flattened as agent sessions instead of preserving
  the `work` -> `try` -> `run` hierarchy from FEAT-010
- Node identity is invisible in the UI — there is no indication of which
  machine or server instance is being viewed

**Desired outcome:** A single `ddx server` UI that surfaces everything
happening on the node. The operator can scan the full bead queue and run
history across all projects, drill into a specific project for artifact-level
work, and bookmark any view with a stable URL.

## URL Structure

All routes embed context so every view is directly addressable:

```
/                                     → redirect to /nodes/:nodeId
/nodes/:nodeId                        → node overview (health, project list)
/nodes/:nodeId/runs                   → combined run history (all projects)
/nodes/:nodeId/beads                  → combined bead queue (all projects)

input.new_string:
## Multi-Machine Sync

DDx is checked out per-project on every machine an operator uses. During an
execute-loop drain, `beads.jsonl` and `executions/` churn every few minutes,
and manually pulling/stashing/pushing across machines is friction that gets
skipped — leading to divergent tracker state.

`ddx sync` is the canonical flow for keeping DDx-managed paths
(`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/plugins/`) aligned with
`origin/main`. Run it as a one-shot (`ddx sync`) or as a daemon
(`ddx sync --watch`) that loops on an interval. The command is constrained
to the DDx-managed allowlist — it never touches paths outside it — and any
condition it cannot auto-resolve (stash-pop conflict, divergent base,
double push failure) aborts cleanly, writes a failure record, and surfaces
through `ddx doctor`.

See **FEAT-023** for the full sync specification.

## How the Pieces Fit

input.old_string:
## Architecture

- **Single binary** — server and web UI (SvelteKit) are embedded in the `ddx` CLI
- **Stateless** — reads from filesystem on each request, no database
- **Localhost by default** — binds to `127.0.0.1` for security
- **File-backed** — all data comes from git-tracked files (`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, library docs, agent logs)
- **Web UI stack** — SvelteKit (Svelte 5, `adapter-static`) + GraphQL (Houdini client) + Bun; built to static files and embedded via `//go:embed`
input.new_string:
## Node Identity and Multi-Node Dashboard

Each `ddx server` instance acquires a stable **node identity** at startup,
derived from `DDX_NODE_NAME` (falling back to the hostname). The node id is
persisted across restarts, and the server maintains a registry of every
project it has been asked to serve — including projects registered by CLI
commands running in other terminals on the same machine. The server writes
an address file so the CLI can find it without configuration. See
**FEAT-020** for the node-state and project-registry specification.

Built on top of node identity, the **multi-node dashboard UI** surfaces
everything happening on the node. Every view embeds node, project, and page
in the URL, so a combined cross-project bead queue, run history, or a
project-scoped artifact browser is bookmarkable and shareable. Run records
preserve the `work` → `try` → `run` hierarchy from the run architecture
rather than flattening to agent sessions. See **FEAT-021** for the
dashboard URL structure and view specifications.

```
/nodes/:nodeId                        → node overview (health, project list)
/nodes/:nodeId/beads                  → combined bead queue (all projects)
/nodes/:nodeId/runs                   → combined run history (all projects)
/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/…  → project-scoped views
```

## Architecture

- **Single binary** — server and web UI (SvelteKit) are embedded in the `ddx` CLI
- **Stateless reads** — documents and beads are read from the filesystem on each request
- **Stateful node identity** — node id, address file, and project registry persist across restarts (FEAT-020)
- **Localhost by default** — binds to `127.0.0.1` for security
- **File-backed** — all data comes from git-tracked files (`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, library docs, agent logs); cross-machine alignment is handled by `ddx sync` (see [Architecture → Multi-Machine Sync](../concepts/architecture/#multi-machine-sync))
- **Web UI stack** — SvelteKit (Svelte 5, `adapter-static`) + GraphQL (Houdini client) + Bun; built to static files and embedded via `//go:embed`
